title: Carissa grandiflora
intro: Carissa grandiflora, also known as the Large-flowered num-num, is a small, evergreen shrub native to the coastal regions of Eastern Africa and South Africa. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its potential health benefits in reducing inflammation and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Carissa grandiflora include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and iridoid glycosides, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Carissa grandiflora include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat fever, respiratory infections, and gastrointestinal disorders.
taxonomy: Carissa grandiflora is classified within the family Apocynaceae, which is a large and diverse family of flowering plants. This family is commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. Carissa grandiflora is further classified within the genus Carissa, which is a small genus of flowering plants that are native to tropical and subtropical regions. The species is often referred to as the Natal plum or Cape chestnut. Carissa grandiflora is native to southern Africa, where it grows as a shrub or small tree.
